---
title: "TerrorMappingWriteup"
author: "Alex Rizzo-Banks"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: 
  html_document:
    code_folding: hide
    number_sections: TRUE
    toc: yes
    toc_depth: 3
    toc_float: yes
  pdf_document:
    toc: yes
    toc_depth: '3'    
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

```{r Read-In}
TerrorData = read.csv(file = "~/GW_ClassStuff/Data_Science_exploration/TerrorDB/TerrorData.csv",)
#"eventid" is structured according to date and then sequential ID of the incident (in case of multiple days), so: YYYYMMDD0001,0002,0003, etc
GTD1 = subset(TerrorData, TerrorData$eventid>200109210000)
#September 21st 2001 is the day after George Bush officially declared the U.S. War on Terror. We'll look at attacks since he said that.
library(dplyr)
GTDalex <- GTD1 %>% select(-c(approxdate,
                           region,
                           latitude, longitude,
                           specificity,
                           alternative,
                           attacktype1, attacktype2, attacktype3,         targtype1, targsubtype1, targtype2, targsubtype2, targtype3, targsubtype3,
                           natlty1, natlty2, natlty3, 
                           gname3, gsubname3, guncertain3, 
                           claimmode, 
                           claim2, claimmode2, claimmode2_txt, 
                           claim3, claimmode3, claimmode3_txt, 
                           weaptype1, weaptype2, weapsubtype1, weapsubtype2,
                  weaptype3, weaptype3_txt, weapsubtype3, weapsubtype3_txt,
                  weaptype4, weaptype4_txt, weapsubtype4, weapsubtype4_txt,
                  nkillus, nwoundus, 
                  propextent, 
          nhostkidus, nhours, ndays, divert, kidhijcountry, hostkidoutcome,
      ransom, ransomamt, ransomamtus, ransompaid, ransompaidus, ransomnote,
                            scite1, scite2, scite3, 
                            INT_ANY))
#We have removed 60 variables to narrow down the dataset for the scope of this class. Information on what was removed will be added later.
colnames(GTDalex) = c(
  "Chronological ID", "Year", "Month", "Day",
  "Attack ran multiple days?", "Date of resolution", 
  "Country code", "Country", "Region", "Province or state", "City", "Attack occurred within city limits?", "Description of location", 
  
  "Description of attack", "Attack meets Criteria 1?", "Attack meets Criteria 2?", "Attack meets Criteria 3?", "Terror label debated?", "Alternate label", "Multiple attacks?", 
  
  "Attack successful?", "Attackers commit suicide?", "Primary mode of attack", "Secondary mode of attack", "Tertiary mode of attack", 
  
  "Target primary type", 
  "Target secondary type", 
  "Targeted entity", 
  "Description of target", 
  "Nationality of target", 
  
  "2nd target primary type",
  "2nd target secondary type", 
  "2nd targeted entity", 
  "Description of 2nd target",
  "Nationality of 2nd target",
  
  "3rd target primary type",
  "3rd target secondary type", 
  "3rd targeted entity", 
  "Description of 3rd target",
  "Nationality of 3rd target",
  
  "Terror group name", "Terror group subname", "2nd terror group name", "2nd terror group subname", "Motive behind attack", "Terror group unconfirmed", "2nd terror group unconfirmed", 
  "Attack by single individual?", "Number of attackers", "Number of attackers captured",
  
  "Group claimed responsibility for attack?", "Mode of claim", "Competing claims?",
  
  "Primary weapon type", "Primary weapon subtype", "Secondary weapon type", "Secondary weapon subtype", "Description of weapon",
  
  "Total number of casualties", "Number of attackers killed", "Total number of wounded", "Number of attackers wounded", 
  "Damage. to property present?", "Level of property damage", "Total value of property damage", "Note on property damage",
  
  "Kidnapping occurred?", "Number kidnapped", "Outcome of kidnapping", "Number of hostages released",
  
  "Misc. notes", "Database used", 
  "Attacker nationality different from country?", "Attacker nationality different from target's?", "Target's nationality different from country?",
  
  "IDs of related attacks"
  )
library(ezids)
library(ggplot2)
#str(GTDalex)
GTD0208 = subset(GTDalex, GTDalex$`Chronological ID`>200201010000 & GTDalex$`Chronological ID`<200812310000)

GTD2014= subset(GTDalex, GTDalex$Year=="2014")

ggplot(GTDalex) + geom_bar(mapping = aes(GTDalex$Year))

ggplot(TerrorData) + geom_bar(mapping = aes(TerrorData$iyear))
```



# How does multiple attacks, weapon type, attack type, month of attack, or region of attack influence whether a terrorist attack causes property damage in 2019?

## Author of this section: Aspen Saplan

## Variable Key:

#### Property:

0 = No

1 = Yes

#### AttackType1:

1 = ASSASSINATION

2 = ARMED ASSAULT

3 = BOMBING/EXPLOSION

4 = HIJACKING

5 = HOSTAGE TAKING (BARRICADE INCIDENT)

6 = HOSTAGE TAKING (KIDNAPPING)

7 = FACILITY / INFRASTRUCTURE ATTACK

8 = UNARMED ASSAULT

9 = UNKNOWN

#### WeaponType1:

1 = Biological

2 = Chemical

3 = Radiological

4 = Nuclear

5 = Firearms

6 = Explosives

7 = Fake Weapons

8 = Incendiary

9 = Melee

10 = Vehicle

11 = Sabotage Equipment

12 = Other

13 =  Unknown

#### Multiple:

0 = No

1 = Yes

#### Month:

1 = Jan

2 = Feb

3 = Mar

4 = April

5 = May

6 = June

7 = July

8 = Aug

9 = Sep

10 = Oct

11 = Nov

12 = Dec

#### Region:

1 = North America

2 = Central America & Caribbean

3 = South America

4 = East Asia

5 = Southeast Asia

6 = South Asia

7 = Central Asia

8 = Western Europe

9 = Eastern Europe

10 = Middle East & North Africa

11 = Sub-Saharan Africa

12 = Australasia & Oceania


## Set up of terror2019 dataset:

Multiple attacks variable represents if the record was one singular terrorist attack or if there were multiple combined that represent one terrorist attack.  The weapon type variable represents what type of weapon was used to perform the terrorist attack.  The month of attack variable represents what month the terrorist attack occurred in.  The region variable represents the region that the terrorist attack took place in.  The property variable is the dependent variable that will be examined and represents whether there was property damage or there was no property damage caused by this terrorist attack.  All of these variables are of the type categorical variable, so the first thing that must be done is as factoring them so that r studio can represent them correctly.  

```{r,echo=TRUE}
terror2019 = read.csv("TerrorData-2019.csv")
# If other datasets are used and include more than years 2019
# terror2019 = terror2019[terror2019$iyear == 2019, ]
terror2019 = terror2019[, c("property", "multiple", "weaptype1", "attacktype1", "imonth", "region")]
terror2019 = terror2019[terror2019$property != -9 & !is.na(terror2019$property),]
terror2019$property = as.factor(terror2019$property)
terror2019$multiple = as.factor(terror2019$multiple)
terror2019$weaptype1 = as.factor(terror2019$weaptype1)
terror2019$attacktype1 = as.factor(terror2019$attacktype1)
terror2019$imonth= as.factor(terror2019$imonth)
terror2019$region = as.factor(terror2019$region)
str(terror2019)
```
## Balance Check:

The next part of exploratory data analysis that is looked at is the ratio of zeros and ones in the property variable in order to know if this variable is balanced.  Which as seen this variable is about fifty four percent no property damage and forty six percent yes property damage.  Which is a pretty good balance of both yes and no values indicating that this is a good dependent variable choice for logistic regression modelling.  

Number of records in 2019 with either property damage or no property damage:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
total = nrow(terror2019)
total
```

Number of records in 2019 without property damage:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
npd = sum(terror2019$property == 0)
npd
```

Number of records in 2019 with property damage:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
pd = sum(terror2019$property == 1)
pd
```

Percentage of records without property damage:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
(npd/total)
```

Percentage of records with property damage:
```{r,echo=TRUE}
(pd/total)
```

## EDA using Bar Charts:

As seen below are all the bar charts that represent the number of property damage compared to each potential covariate.  The red bars represent no property damage, while the purple bars represent yes property damage.

For multiple terrorist attacks, no property damage is favored for single terrorist attacks, while yes property damage is favored for multiple terrorist attacks.  Although, the single terrorist attack overall has more values then multiple terrorist attacks.  

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = multiple, fill = property)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black")+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "1" = "purple",
    "0" = "red"
  ))+
  labs(title = "Count of Property Damage by Multiple Attacks",
    x = "Multiple",
    y = "Count of Property Damage",
    fill = "Property Damage")
```

The weapon type variable shows a mix of having more yes property damage and no property damage.  What is interesting though is the insight on which weapons are used more often in 2019 for a terrorist attack.  Overall, weapon five, six, eight, and thirteen make up the majority of weapon types used compared to all the rest.  Some weapon types did not even occur in 2019 such as weapon three and four.  

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = weaptype1, fill = property)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black")+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "1" = "purple",
    "0" = "red"
  ))+
  labs(title = "Count of Property Damage by Weapon Type",
    x = "Weapon Type",
    y = "Count of Property Damage",
    fill = "Property Damage")
```

The attack type variable shows similar results to the weapon type variable in the sense that some attacks caused more property damage then not.  While some attacks caused more no property damage than actual property damage.  

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = attacktype1, fill = property)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black")+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "1" = "purple",
    "0" = "red"
  ))+
  labs(title = "Count of Property Damage by Attack Type",
    x = "Attack Type",
    y = "Count of Property Damage",
    fill = "Property Damage")
```

The month of attack variable has pretty similar results across each month.  Some months such as month twelve or month four have a spike in no property damage, while most of the other months have equal amounts of yes property damage and no property damage.

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = imonth, fill = property)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black")+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "1" = "purple",
    "0" = "red"
  ))+
  labs(title = "Count of Property Damage by Month of Attack",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Count of Property Damage",
    fill = "Property Damage")
```

The region variable shows that some regions have a large amount of both no property damage and yes property damage such as regions six, ten, and eleven.  While the other regions have very little amount of terrorist attacks as a whole such as month four or twelve.

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(terror2019, aes(x = region, fill = property)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge", color = "black")+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "1" = "purple",
    "0" = "red"
  ))+
  labs(title = "Count of Property Damage by Region of Attack",
    x = "Region",
    y = "Count of Property Damage",
    fill = "Property Damage")
```

## Hypothesis Testing:

The next step of exploratory data analysis used is the hypothesis test on each potential covariate and the dependent variable or property.  Since all the covariates are categorical variable types and property is a categorical variable type a chi squared test of independence can be used for all of them.   Each null hypothesis is that the potential covariate and the property variable are independent of each other.  Each alternative hypothesis is that the potential covariate and the property variable are not independent of each other.  The alpha value that will be accepted is 0.05.

Null Hypothesis: Multiple attacks and property damage are independent

Alternative Hypothesis: Multiple attacks and property damage are not independent

Alpha: 0.05

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ezids)
contableM = table(terror2019$property, terror2019$multiple)
xkabledply(contableM, title="Contingency table for property and multiple within dataset")
chitestM = chisq.test(contableM)
chitestM
```

Null Hypothesis: Weapon type and property damage are independent

Alternative Hypothesis: Weapon type and property damage are not independent

Alpha: 0.05

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ezids)
contableW = table(terror2019$property, terror2019$weaptype1)
xkabledply(contableW, title="Contingency table for property and weapon type within dataset")
chitestW = chisq.test(contableW)
chitestW
```

Null Hypothesis: Attack type and property damage are independent

Alternative Hypothesis: Attack type and property damage are not independent

Alpha: 0.05

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ezids)
contableA = table(terror2019$property, terror2019$attacktype1)
xkabledply(contableA, title="Contingency table for property and attack type within dataset")
chitestA = chisq.test(contableA)
chitestA
```

Null Hypothesis: Month of attack and property damage are independent

Alternative Hypothesis: Month of attack and property damage are not independent

Alpha: 0.05

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ezids)
contableMO = table(terror2019$property, terror2019$imonth)
xkabledply(contableMO, title="Contingency table for property and month of attack within dataset")
chitestMO = chisq.test(contableMO)
chitestMO
```

Null Hypothesis: Region of attack and property damage are independent

Alternative Hypothesis: Region of attack and property damage are not independent

Alpha: 0.05

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(ezids)
contableR = table(terror2019$property, terror2019$region)
xkabledply(contableR, title="Contingency table for property and region of attack within dataset")
chitestR = chisq.test(contableR)
chitestR
```
Each potential covariate being multiple, weapon type, attack type, region, month have p-values that are less than our set alpha value.  This indicates that for each chi squared test we can reject the null hypotheses, indicating that each potential covariate is not independent of property.

## Logistic Regression Model 1:

### glm(property ~ multiple + attacktype1 + imonth + region + weaptype1, data = terror2019, family = binomial):

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(stats)
model1 = glm(property ~ multiple + attacktype1 + imonth + region + weaptype1, data = terror2019, family = binomial)
summary(model1)
```

### Accuracy:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
probs = predict(model1, type = "response")
pred = ifelse(probs > 0.5, 1, 0)
table(Predicted = pred, Actual= terror2019$property)
mean(pred == terror2019$property)
```

### ROC and AUC:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(pROC)
probs = predict(model1, type = "response")
r = roc(terror2019$property, probs)
plot(r)
auc(r)
best <- coords(r, "best", ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity"), best.method = "youden")
best
```

### Deviance:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
p1 = pchisq(8958.7 - 7069.4, df = 40, lower.tail = FALSE)
p1
```

The first logistic regression model includes all the variables that could be covariates.  Looking at the coefficients the multiple, attack type, month, and region variables have statistically significance or lower than 0.05 p-values.  Weapon type does not have any statistical significance so it can be removed in the next model.  The accuracy for this model is approximately seventy one percent.  The auc for this model is about seventy eight point five percent.  Thus, this model is looking like it has pretty good results.  However, the deviance for this model is exactly zero.  This represents that this model is a perfect fit, which is never truly the case.  So even though this looked like a good model, it is most likely due to over fitting the data which is causing it to be a perfect model.

## Logistic Regression Model 2:

### glm(property ~ multiple + attacktype1 + imonth, data = terror2019, family = binomial)

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(stats)
model2 = glm(property ~ multiple + attacktype1 + imonth, data = terror2019, family = binomial)
summary(model2)
```

### Accuracy:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
probs1 = predict(model2, type = "response")
pred1 = ifelse(probs1 > 0.5, 1, 0)
table(Predicted = pred1, Actual= terror2019$property)
mean(pred1 == terror2019$property)
```
### ROC and AUC

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(pROC)
probs1 = predict(model2, type = "response")
r = roc(terror2019$property, probs1)
plot(r)
auc(r)
best <- coords(r, "best", ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity"), best.method = "youden")
best
```

### Deviance:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
p2 = pchisq(8958.7 - 7616.2, df = 20, lower.tail = FALSE)
p2
```

Through different testing of plugging and chugging different sets of independent variables, model two is the one that showed the best results while also not being overfitted with data.  The variables that were chosen to be left in as covariates include multiple, attack type, and month variables.  This produced a model that has an aic of 7658.2, which was lower than any other model that was tested on.  Model two has an accuracy of about sixty seven point two percent.  Model two’s auc is about seventy three percent.  While model two’s deviance is less than 0.05, but not exactly zero indicating that it is statistically better than the null and saturated models and that it is not overfitting the data. 

Looking at each covariate it can be seen that not every type within a covariate is statistically significant.  One example being that attack type three, four, five, six, seven and nine are statistically significant when compared to attack type one.  However, attack type two and eight are not statistically significant compared to attack type one.  Also, only month two, four, and twelve have statistical significance when compared to month one for this logistic regression model.

### Odds Ratio:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
exp(coef(model2))
```

Looking at the odds ratios of each coefficient it can be seen that some variables are above one, some below one, and some very close to one.  This indicates for variables like multiple when compared to its reference variable has a sixty six percent increase in odds of property damage happening.  While variables like month three when compared to its reference variable have a twelve percent decrease in odds of property damage happening.  

## Logistic Regression Model 3(Just for comparison):

### glm(property ~ multiple + attacktype1, data = terror2019, family = binomial):

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(stats)
model3 = glm(property ~ multiple + attacktype1, data = terror2019, family = binomial)
summary(model3)
```

### Accuracy:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
probs2 = predict(model3, type = "response")
pred2 = ifelse(probs2 > 0.5, 1, 0)
table(Predicted = pred2, Actual= terror2019$property)
mean(pred2 == terror2019$property)
```

### ROC and AUC:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
library(pROC)
probs2 = predict(model3, type = "response")
r = roc(terror2019$property, probs2)
plot(r)
auc(r)
best <- coords(r, "best", ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity"), best.method = "youden")
best
```

### Deviance:

```{r,echo=TRUE}
p2 = pchisq(8958.7 - 7664.5, df = 11, lower.tail = FALSE)
p2
```

The inclusion of this third model is just to give a comparison of why model two was selected as the best model.  Model 2 has a better predictive accuracy than model 3.  Model 2 also has a lower aic than model 3 indicating it is a better model even with model 2 getting penalized for having more independent variables.  Model 2 also has a higher auc than model 3 indicating that model 2 is better at distinguishing the different cases than model 3.

## Conclusion:

To conclude, depending on the number of attacks within one terrorist attack, the type of terrorist attack, and the month in which the terrorist attack occurs can either increase or decrease the odds that your property will have damage after the terrorist attack.  Those three variables are the most influential variables on likelihood of property damage.  So, if for some reason a terrorist attack happened in 2019 and I happened to be there, I would hope that it would be one attack of type six which is a hostage taking or kidnapping, and is in April so that I would have the lowest odds of having damage to my car or house.



---
title: "War on Terror project 2 Ali Mutasim"
output: html_document
date: "2026-04-16"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r}

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(MASS)     
library(rpart)   
library(rpart.plot)
```

libraries were imported to do data manipulation, visualization, regression modeling, feature selection, and decsision trees. 


```{r}
Terror <- read.csv("TerrorData.csv")

Terror$iyear <- as.numeric(Terror$iyear)
Terror$nkill <- as.numeric(Terror$nkill)
Terror$nwound <- as.numeric(Terror$nwound)
Terror$propvalue <- as.numeric(Terror$propvalue)

Terror$nkill[is.na(Terror$nkill)] <- 0
Terror$nwound[is.na(Terror$nwound)] <- 0
Terror$propvalue[is.na(Terror$propvalue)] <- 0
```

the variables are converted into numeric form so that R can analzye and calculate well. it replaces NA values with zeros so that errors aren't caused.


```{r}
Terror$casualties <- Terror$nkill + Terror$nwound
Terror$log_casualties <- log1p(Terror$casualties)
Terror$log_prop <- log1p(Terror$propvalue)

Terror$period <- ifelse(Terror$iyear < 2001, "Pre-2001", "Post-2001")
Terror$period <- as.factor(Terror$period)
```
here number of killed and wounded into one variable called casualties. variable to show severity. log transforamtion is used to make them suitable for regression modeling. a variable period is created so that attacks are compared pre and post 2001.

```{r}
attacks_per_year <- Terror %>%
  group_by(iyear) %>%
  summarise(n_attacks = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(period = ifelse(iyear < 2001, "Pre-2001", "Post-2001"))

ggplot(attacks_per_year, aes(x = iyear, y = n_attacks)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2001, linetype="dashed")

ggplot(attacks_per_year, aes(x = period, y = n_attacks)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill="lightblue")

t.test(n_attacks ~ period, data = attacks_per_year)
```


Plots are created here by grouping attacks in each year and seeing how the frequency of these attaks change over time. a line plot is created to show how the number of attacks changes over time. a boxplot is created to show the frequency of attacks pre and post 2001 or 9/11


```{r}
# Scatterplot: casualties vs year
ggplot(Terror, aes(x = iyear, y = casualties)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")

# Correlation matrix (numeric only)
numeric_vars <- Terror[, c("iyear","nkill","nwound","propvalue","casualties")]
cor(numeric_vars, use="complete.obs")

```
This step involves two-variable graphics to examine the relationships in the data. To determine whether the intensity of attacks varies with time a scatterplot of year vs casualties is plotted. The numeric variables are also correlated to one another, so that you can learn whether or not the variables move together, and whether there are any strong linear associations among them.


```{r}
model_full <- lm(log_casualties ~ iyear + attacktype1_txt + region_txt + period,
                 data = Terror)

summary(model_full)
```

A regression model is then constructed to describe the severity of attacks using the script. In this model, the dependent variable will be the log-transformed number of casualties, with the predictors being year, type of attack, region and period. This enables you to test the relationship between that and the other factors in the model and the other factors that are associated with more severe attacks.


```{r}
model_step <- stepAIC(model_full, direction = "both")

summary(model_step)
```
Once the entire regression model has been fitted, the script will select features by relying on stepAIC. The process eliminates variables which do not contribute to the model to the extent desired and retains the combination of predictors which offers the best tradeoff between model fit and simplicity. This is useful in creating a model that is less complicated and can be more efficient.


```{r}
# R-squared
summary(model_step)$r.squared

# Adjusted R-squared
summary(model_step)$adj.r.squared

# ANOVA comparison
anova(model_step)
```

After the last model has been selected, the script analyses the performance of the model. It analyzes the value of the R-squared and adjusted R-squared to understand the extent of the variation in the severity of the attack explained by the model. It also compares ANOVA output to determine the significance of the terms fitted in the model.

```{r}
par(mfrow=c(1,1))
plot(model_step)
```

Improper assumptions of the regression model are then verified using the regression model on the basis of diagnostic plots. These plots assist you to observe whether or not the residual values are approximately normal, the variance is reasonably constant, and whether there are influential points that may substantially affect the outcomes. This is significant in the sense that when the assumptions of the regression are moderately satisfied, the conclusions of the regression are more trustworthy.

```{r}
model_prop <- lm(log_prop ~ iyear + attacktype1_txt + region_txt + period,
                 data = Terror)

summary(model_prop)
```

The script then makes a second regression model with log-transformed property damage as a dependent variable. The reason behind this is that the severity that your project is gauging on is not only based on casualties, but also on property damages, which is the second outcome. This model assists in determining what variables are related to more harmful attacks in terms of property damage.

```{r}
tree_model <- rpart(log_casualties ~ iyear + attacktype1_txt + region_txt + period,
                    data = Terror,
                    method = "anova")

rpart.plot(tree_model)
```
A decision tree model of casualties is also provided in the script. A decision tree is more flexible in capturing nonlinear patterns and interactions than linear regression. This provides you another modeling method to contrast the regression results and makes your project stronger in the technical aspect.

```{r}
chisq.test(table(Terror$attacktype1_txt, Terror$period))
chisq.test(table(Terror$region_txt, Terror$period))

```

Lastly, chi-square tests are employed in the script to examine the change in categorical patterns with time. One chi-square test is used to test whether the distribution of attacks type differs between the pre-2001 and the post-2001 period, and another one to test whether the regional distribution of attacks varies across the period. These tests are useful in ascertaining whether patterns of terrorism changed in terms of structure following 9/11, and not merely in terms of frequency or intensity.
Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.
